items.q: What does dependency theory argue about development?
items.correct: Poorer 'periphery' countries are kept underdeveloped by their economic relationships with rich 'core' countries — through unequal trade, debt and historical colonialism. It challenges the view that integration with rich economies automatically helps.
items.misconception: Dependency theory means poor countries are simply lazy.
items.random_wrong: Dependency theory is about households relying on benefits.
items.why: Dependency theory is a structural critique of global capitalism, not a behavioural claim about poor countries. Students sometimes invert the analysis entirely or apply 'dependency' to individuals rather than nations.

items.q: What are the functions of money?
items.correct: Medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account, and standard of deferred payment — money must perform these to function as money.
items.misconception: Money is valuable because it is made of valuable materials.
items.random_wrong: Money is only valuable if backed by gold.
items.why: Modern fiat money has no intrinsic value — its value comes from social acceptance and government issuance. Students think paper money 'must' be backed by something; it isn't, and hasn't been since 1971.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.
Correct answer: Inflation rate. “Life expectancy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Freedoms people have to live valued lives
items.why: It's Freedoms people have to live valued lives. “GDP growth alone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many indicators does the HDI use?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
